root_causes.type: Physical-layer receive errors (cabling / SFP / remote port)
root_causes.cause: Inbound (RX) frame errors on Gi0/0/1 of router1 and router2 consistent with a physical-layer fault on those links — e.g. bad cable, failing SFP/port, or intermittent receive-side hardware error on the link partner.
evidence: router1 GigabitEthernet0/0/1: input_errors=28, output_errors=0, drops=0, admin=up oper=up (last_change=2026-05-28T12:55:52Z)
router2 GigabitEthernet0/0/1: input_errors=31, output_errors=0, drops=0, admin=up oper=up (last_change=2026-05-28T12:55:50Z)
Errors are limited to input side (no output_errors, no drops) which commonly indicates receive/physical-layer problems.

root_causes.cause: Auto-negotiation or duplex mismatch between the routers and their link partners could produce receive errors/alignments; duplex information is not reported (duplex=null) so a negotiation problem cannot be ruled out.
evidence: Tool output shows duplex=null for both interfaces (no negotiated duplex reported).
Both interfaces show non-zero input_errors while remaining up — a pattern seen with duplex/negotiation problems.

root_causes.type: Shared upstream device or simultaneous event
root_causes.cause: A common upstream device (switch/patch panel) or a simultaneous event/change around 12:55:50–12:55:52Z affected both links and produced RX errors on R1 and R2.
evidence: router1 last_change=2026-05-28T12:55:52.000491+00:00 and router2 last_change=2026-05-28T12:55:50.000117+00:00 — near-identical timestamps indicating a concurrent event.
Both routers show similar symptom (input_errors present) at the same timeframe, suggesting a shared cause.

root_causes.cause: Speed/duplex/negotiation mismatch between router1 and router2 interfaces causing receive errors and unstable link.
evidence: R1 running-config: Cisco-IOS-XE-ethernet:negotiation: auto = true (interface GigabitEthernet0/0/1)
R2 running-config: Cisco-IOS-XE-ethernet:negotiation: auto = false; Cisco-IOS-XE-ethernet:speed = value-100; duplex = half (interface GigabitEthernet0/0/1)
R1 interface status: input_errors = 28 (oper_state = lower-layer-down)
R2 interface status: input_errors = 31 (oper_state = down, speed reported = 102400000)

root_causes.cause: Router2 GigabitEthernet0/0/1 is administratively down which results in the peer (router1) seeing lower-layer/down and contributes to RX errors and OSPF interface Down.
evidence: R2 interface status: admin_state = down, oper_state = down, input_errors = 31
R1 interface status: admin_state = up, oper_state = lower-layer-down, input_errors = 28
OSPF on both routers shows GigabitEthernet0/0/1 interface_state = Down

root_causes.cause: Residual physical issue (SFP/cable/connector) could still be producing receive errors despite replacements, but evidence is inconclusive.
evidence: Both interfaces show non-zero input_errors (R1=28, R2=31) even after descriptions indicate replacements (running-config descriptions: 'replaced_sfp' / previous_changes show 'replaced_cable')
No CRC error counter provided (crc_errors = null) — physical error counters not shown in current outputs, so hardware fault cannot be confirmed or ruled out

data.intent_description: GigabitEthernet0/0/1 on router1 and router2 show high input_errors and the link is down due to R2 being administratively down and a speed/duplex/negotiation mismatch; remediate to restore an error-free, up link.

plan.problem: R2 GigabitEthernet0/0/1 is administratively down and configured with fixed speed=100/duplex=half while R1 is set to auto-negotiation, causing input_errors and link down on both routers.
plan.fix_summary: Bring R2 Gi0/0/1 admin state up and configure the interface to use auto-negotiation (clear fixed speed/duplex) so both ends match. This should stop RX errors and restore the link.

plan.fix_summary: Bring R2 Gi0/0/1 admin state up and configure the interface to use auto-negotiation (clear fixed speed/duplex) so both ends match. This should stop RX errors and restore the link.

remaining_issues: R2 GigabitEthernet0/0/1 still configured with fixed speed=100 and duplex=half (negotiation auto = false) per running-config — configuration mismatch with R1 (which is set to auto-negotiation).
Non-zero input_errors remain on both interfaces (R1=28, R2=31), indicating residual receive errors despite the operational link and OSPF adjacency.

Running-config evidence indicates the planned change to enable auto-negotiation was not applied (or not persisted) on R2, so the original root cause (speed/duplex negotiation mismatch) is not verifiably resolved.

messages.content:
What was observed
- R1 and R2 GigabitEthernet0/0/1 showed receive errors (R1 input_errors=28, R2 input_errors=31). R1 oper=lower-layer-down when R2 was admin down. R1 is set to auto-negotiation; R2 was configured speed=100/duplex=half and admin down.

Diagnosis (main cause)
- Primary: speed/duplex/negotiation mismatch between R1 and R2 plus R2 interface being administratively down. These caused RX errors and the OSPF interface to be Down.

Changes made
- Brought interfaces up on both routers (R1 and R2 Gi0/0/1).
- Installed/recorded known-good SFP and cable (descriptions set on both interfaces).
- Added descriptions noting "speed=auto duplex=auto per remediation plan" on R2 (informational only).

Verification — result
- Partial: Interface operational state and OSPF adjacency are now up on both routers (passed).
- Failed: R2 running-config still shows negotiation auto = false; speed = 100; duplex = half (the fixed speed/duplex was not actually removed). Non-zero input_errors remain on both sides (R1=28, R2=31). Root cause (negotiation mismatch) is therefore not resolved and physical-layer faults have not been ruled out.

Recommended next steps
- Apply/remove the fixed speed/duplex on R2 so negotiation=auto (or explicitly configure both ends the same), then verify input_errors drop and link remains stable.
- If errors persist after negotiation matches, collect CRC/error counters and replace SFP/cable or test hardware.
